ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chicken feet specifically; A0CKN (chicken meat, raw) is the closest defensible parent code. Chicken feet are a distinct anatomical part (foot including skin, bone, cartilage) and differ compositionally from breast or thigh meat.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for raw chicken feet as a food item; omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw chicken feet is approximately 100 g, equivalent to 3–4 whole feet, commonly used as a single portion in braised or dim sum preparations.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken feet, raw — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chicken feet, raw (F11010026). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate animal protein food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chicken feet, raw contains only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI testing is performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (67 mg/100 g) in raw state; saturated fat 3.9 g/100 g is below the 5 g/100 g threshold; potassium is very low at 31 mg/100 g providing minimal vasodilatory offset
hypertension.guidance: Raw chicken feet are low in sodium and suitable for hypertension management in their uncooked state, but patients should be strongly cautioned that common preparations such as braising with soy sauce, oyster sauce, or black bean sauce will dramatically increase sodium content and should be requested with low-sodium sauces or consumed in small portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.2 g/100 g), zero sugar, and high protein (19.4 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact from the collagen-rich composition
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw chicken feet have a negligible glycaemic impact due to near-zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content, making them suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, patients should avoid preparations with sweet sauces (hoisin, sweet soy) or oyster sauce that add significant carbohydrate and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (14.6 g/100 g) with saturated fat at 3.9 g/100 g and cholesterol at 84 mg/100 g; low sodium in raw state but high-sodium cooking preparations are common
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw chicken feet have moderate cardiovascular concern due to their total fat and cholesterol content; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose steaming or boiling over deep-frying, avoid high-sodium braising sauces, and note that the skin contributes the majority of saturated fat and cholesterol.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No positive anti-inflammatory factors present — no omega-3, no polyphenols, no antioxidant-rich nutrients, no dietary fibre, and no fermented components; collagen content does not meet the RA positive-criterion requirement
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw chicken feet do not meet the positive anti-inflammatory criteria required for RA suitability; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should pair chicken feet with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger, garlic, and turmeric, and avoid deep-frying or high-sodium braising preparations that may worsen systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken feet, raw, are the uncooked foot portions of chicken, comprising skin, bone, cartilage, tendons, and a small amount of muscle tissue, with a characteristic light yellow colour. They are a widely consumed ingredient across Chinese, Southeast Asian, and Caribbean cuisines, most commonly prepared by braising, steaming (as in dim sum phoenix claws / feng zhua), deep-frying followed by braising, or slow-cooking in soups and stocks. The raw texture is firm and gelatinous when cooked due to the high collagen content, which dissolves into a rich, sticky mouthfeel. Nutritionally, raw chicken feet provide 215 kcal per 100 g, with 19.4 g protein, 14.6 g fat (saturated fat approximately 3.9 g), and only 0.2 g carbohydrate, making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 67 mg/100 g in the raw state, though prepared dishes are typically high in sodium. Calcium is notably present at 88 mg/100 g, contributed by the bone and cartilage matrix. Cholesterol is 84 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, raw chicken feet are suitable for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management in their uncooked state, but cooking method selection is critical as braising sauces dramatically alter the sodium and sugar profile. They are not recommended as a standalone food for rheumatoid arthritis due to the absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients. In Singapore, chicken feet are commonly found in dim sum restaurants as feng zhua (鳳爪) and in hawker centres as braised chicken feet side dishes.

Is chicken feet good for diabetics?
Can hypertension patients eat chicken feet?
Is chicken feet high in collagen?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11010026) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. The food identity is unambiguous and the source match is exact.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for raw chicken feet are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11010026 as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 05358, Chicken, feet, raw) reports monounsaturated fat approximately 6.0 g and polyunsaturated fat approximately 3.1 g per 100 g as a cross-reference estimate, but these values are not adopted here as the primary source is HPB and the USDA entry may reflect different breed or processing conditions. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g) — naturally zero as this is an animal protein food with no plant-derived fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per the q4 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Saturated fat value of 3.9 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05358 as a cross-reference since HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value. The food is classified as halal-suitable in its raw state subject to halal slaughter certification. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language values confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases array.
